summary: Gruber examines the paradox of the Mac's market renaissance coinciding with unprecedented doubt about its long-term future. While Mac sales have tripled and first-time buyers are flooding in, the iPad has already outsold the Mac just six months after launch. He argues that Apple's well-known habit of killing legacy technology doesn't apply to the Mac yet because iOS actually depends on the Mac's existence — the Mac carries the 'baggage' of complex tasks so iOS can remain light and elegant. Rather than iOS replacing the Mac, the two platforms exist in a symbiotic relationship where the Mac's heaviness enables iOS's simplicity.
key_points: The Mac is experiencing a rare market renaissance with tripling sales and majority first-time buyers, yet faces more doubt about its future than at any point since Jobs's return in 1997
Apple's cultural aversion to legacy technology stems from fear of being weighed down — they figure out how to get rid of old tech, not carry it forward
The Mac isn't slowing iOS down; the opposite is true — Mac OS X development was delayed to shift engineering resources to the iPhone
The Mac's continued existence allows iOS to do less, which is the iPad's central conceit: doing less means doing things better, more simply, and more elegantly
iOS can only replace the Mac if and when it expands to handle everything the Mac does, which would undermine its core advantage
money_quotes: The irony is that there's more doubt today about the long-term prospects of the Mac than there has been at any time since Steve Jobs returned to Apple in 1997.

Rather than suffering defeat at the hands of a competing platform — like, say, Microsoft Windows — the problem for the Mac today is that it has been overshadowed by its own sibling, the fabulously precocious iOS.

Apple's cultural aversion to legacy technology isn't about a lack of seriousness, or a short companywide attention span. It's not about being attracted only to the new and shiny. It's about fear — the fear of being weighed down by excess baggage.
At typical companies, "legacy" technology is something they figure out how to carry forward. At Apple, legacy technology is something they figure out how to get rid of.
The central conceit of the iPad is that it's a portable computer that does less — and because it does less, what it does do, it does better, more simply, and more elegantly.
When I say that iOS has no baggage, that's not because there is no baggage. It's because the Mac is there to carry it.
It's the heaviness of the Mac that allows iOS to remain light.
key_insight: The Mac and iOS exist in symbiosis: the Mac carries the complexity so iOS can stay light, meaning Apple can't phase out the Mac without undermining what makes iOS great.
